questions.q: What does ACID stand for in database transactions?
options: Architecture, Consistency, Integration, Distribution
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
Authorization, Concurrency, Index, Design
Aggregation, Clustering, Integrity, Data
questions.explanation: ACID is the acronym for four properties guaranteeing reliable database transactions. Atomicity ensures all-or-nothing execution, consistency maintains valid state, isolation prevents interference, and durability persists committed data.

questions.q: What does atomicity mean in a database transaction?
options: Transactions execute on a single processor core
All operations in a transaction complete successfully or none of them do
Transactions cannot read uncommitted changes from other transactions
Committed data is permanently stored and survives failures
questions.explanation: Atomicity guarantees 'all-or-nothing' execution: either all changes commit or the entire transaction rolls back. This prevents partial updates that leave data in an inconsistent state. Isolation and durability are separate ACID properties.

questions.q: What isolation level allows dirty reads?
options: SERIALIZABLE
REPEATABLE READ
READ COMMITTED
READ UNCOMMITTED
questions.explanation: READ UNCOMMITTED permits dirty reads of uncommitted data from other transactions. This is the weakest isolation level, offering the best performance but lowest data integrity. SERIALIZABLE provides the strongest isolation.

options: The transaction executes only when the database is not busy
Data maintains valid state according to defined rules and constraints
Two transactions can modify the same row simultaneously
Transactions complete in the order they start
questions.explanation: Consistency ensures all database constraints (foreign keys, unique constraints, check constraints) are satisfied before and after a transaction. A transaction takes the database from one valid state to another valid state.

options: Transactions will never fail or roll back
Committed data persists even after system crashes or failures
No one can delete committed data
All queries execute faster than uncommitted queries
questions.explanation: Durability ensures that once a transaction commits, the changes survive hardware failures, power loss, and crashes. Databases use write-ahead logs and persistent storage to guarantee this property.

questions.q: What problem does the REPEATABLE READ isolation level prevent?
options: Dirty reads and phantom reads but allows non-repeatable reads
Dirty reads and non-repeatable reads but allows phantom reads
Only dirty reads
Dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads
questions.explanation: REPEATABLE READ prevents dirty reads and non-repeatable reads (re-reading the same row returns consistent results). It allows phantom reads where new rows inserted by other transactions appear. SERIALIZABLE prevents all three anomalies.

options: Reading data that was never committed to the database
A row changing its values during a transaction
New rows inserted by other transactions appearing in a repeated query
Reading data deleted by another transaction
questions.explanation: A phantom read occurs when a query returns different rows in two consecutive executions due to inserts or deletes by other transactions between the queries. Locking a range prevents this; SERIALIZABLE isolation prevents phantom reads.

options: When the database runs out of disk space
When an error occurs, constraints are violated, or the application requests ROLLBACK
When a query takes longer than expected to execute
Every transaction rolls back automatically after 10 minutes
questions.explanation: A transaction rolls back to undo all changes when errors occur, constraints are violated, or the application explicitly calls ROLLBACK. This maintains atomicity and prevents partial updates from corrupting data.

questions.q: What is the tradeoff between stronger isolation levels and performance?
options: Stronger isolation always improves performance
Stronger isolation provides better data integrity but requires more locking, reducing concurrency
Weaker isolation levels are always slower
Isolation levels have no performance impact
questions.explanation: Stricter isolation (SERIALIZABLE) uses more locks and blocks concurrent transactions, reducing throughput. Weaker isolation (READ UNCOMMITTED) allows concurrent access but risks data anomalies. The choice depends on consistency requirements vs. performance needs.

options: A transaction that reads from multiple tables
The same query returns different data in two executions during a transaction because another transaction modified the rows
Reading data before it is committed
A row that cannot be read by any user
questions.explanation: A non-repeatable read occurs when reading the same row twice within a transaction yields different values due to updates by other transactions. READ COMMITTED and weaker levels allow this; REPEATABLE READ and SERIALIZABLE prevent it.
